---
title: "Exercise: Bundling outputs into a `.zip`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write a function that bundles several named blobs into a single `.zip` archive:

```rust
fn write_zip_bundle(output_path: &Path, entries: &[(&str, &[u8])]) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>
```

The function should also support reading entries back, via a sibling function `read_zip_bundle`. This is how you would hand a collaborator a complete per-sample QC report: one TSV of stats, one histogram PNG, one summary text file, all in one downloadable file.

## Gzip vs zip — the one rule

The two compressed formats biologists use look superficially similar but are not the same thing:

| Format | What it is | Tool |
|--------|----------|------|
| `.gz` / `.bgz` / `.bgzf` | **one** compressed byte stream | [`flate2`](https://docs.rs/flate2/), [`noodles-bgzf`](https://docs.rs/noodles-bgzf/) |
| `.zip` | **archive** of named entries, each compressed | [`zip`](https://docs.rs/zip/) |

`.fa.gz`, `.fastq.gz`, `.vcf.gz` are all the first kind: a single sequence file with one compressed body. A `.zip` is the second kind: a directory's worth of separate files. This exercise is about the second kind.

## The crate

```toml
[dependencies]
zip = "0.6"
```

You need:

- [`zip::ZipWriter::new(Write)`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/write/struct.ZipWriter.html#method.new) to start an archive
- [`zip::write::FileOptions::default()`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/write/struct.FileOptions.html) — default compression settings
- [`zw.start_file(name, options)`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/write/struct.ZipWriter.html#method.start_file) to begin a new entry
- [`std::io::Write::write_all`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/trait.Write.html#method.write_all) to write the entry's bytes
- [`zw.finish()`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/write/struct.ZipWriter.html#method.finish) to close the archive

For reading:

- [`zip::ZipArchive::new(Read + Seek)`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/read/struct.ZipArchive.html#method.new) to open an archive
- [`archive.by_index(i)`](https://docs.rs/zip/0.6/zip/read/struct.ZipArchive.html#method.by_index) to access entry `i`
- The returned `ZipFile` implements [`Read`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/trait.Read.html), so `read_to_end` etc. work on it.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day4/ex-zip-bundle
```

Implement both `write_zip_bundle` and `read_zip_bundle`. Make all three tests pass — they round-trip a few small entries through an in-memory `Cursor`, then through a real temp file on disk.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — write side"}
```rust
let mut zw = zip::ZipWriter::new(file_or_cursor);
let options = zip::write::FileOptions::default();
for (name, body) in entries {
    zw.start_file(*name, options)?;
    zw.write_all(body)?;
}
zw.finish()?;
```

`start_file` takes the entry name as `&str` and the options struct.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — read side"}
```rust
let mut archive = zip::ZipArchive::new(reader)?;
let mut out: Vec<(String, Vec<u8>)> = Vec::with_capacity(archive.len());
for i in 0..archive.len() {
    let mut entry = archive.by_index(i)?;
    let name = entry.name().to_string();
    let mut body = Vec::new();
    entry.read_to_end(&mut body)?;
    out.push((name, body));
}
Ok(out)
```

`archive.len()` is the number of entries. `by_index` returns one entry at a time (you cannot hold two at once because reading the archive moves an internal cursor).
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day4/ex-zip-bundle-solution/src/main.rs` for the full reference.
:::

## Where this leads

- Real production reports often use [`tar` + gzip](https://docs.rs/tar/) instead of zip — same idea, different file format. Pick whichever your downstream tooling expects.
- This is the natural endpoint for a day-4 capstone: stream FASTQ records with [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/), compute QC stats, render a histogram with [`plotters`](https://docs.rs/plotters/), and bundle the TSV + PNG into one `.zip` for handover.
